course_subtitle: How new optical approaches address scale, efficiency, density, latency, and compute integration in AI infrastructure.

sources.path: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

sources.title: US Conec PRIZM Expanded Beam Ferrules
Nature Photonics hollow-core fiber attenuation paper

term_glossary.tooltip: Co-packaged optics: optical engines located close to the switching ASIC to reduce electrical reach and improve density.
Near-packaged optics: optics moved close to, but not fully inside, the switch package.
Areal Packaged Optics: a future-facing integration approach where optical I/O can escape through the die face rather than only around the die or package perimeter.
Expanded beam optics: connector designs that use lenses to expand and collimate the beam, reducing sensitivity to small contaminants at the mating interface.
Mean time to turn up: the elapsed time required to bring a deployed network or cluster into stable production service.
A lensed expanded-beam ferrule approach used here as a peer example to EBO for non-contact, debris-tolerant optical interfaces.
term_glossary.reference_label: US Conec PRIZM expanded beam ferrules

term_glossary.tooltip: Multicore fiber: a fiber with multiple independent cores inside a shared cladding.
Hollow-core fiber: optical fiber that guides light mostly through air rather than through a solid glass core.
Quantum processing unit: a specialized accelerator for quantum circuits in a hybrid computing system.

slides.title: AI Fabrics Are Becoming Optical-First Systems
slides.subtitle: Higher-speed switches and larger fabrics turn fiber infrastructure into a system-level constraint.

bullets.text: AI back-end fabrics are already treating 800G as a common design point, with 1.6T deployments ramping.

bullets.bold_lead: Cluster scale changes the optics problem
bullets.text: More endpoints, more switch radix, and more rack-scale links multiply fiber paths and connector events.
Faceplate density, power, thermals, and service access become harder as port counts and fiber counts rise.

bullets.bold_lead: The cable plant is part of performance
bullets.text: Tray fill, bend management, testability, and cleanliness now affect turn-up time and usable capacity.

slides.title: Traditional Data Centers vs AI Data Centers
slides.subtitle: AI clusters create different traffic, failure, and density assumptions than conventional enterprise workloads.

cards.title: Traditional model
cards.body: Many independent applications, moderate east-west coupling, and network issues that often affect smaller service domains.

cards.title: AI model
cards.body: Large synchronized training or inference systems, dense accelerator fabrics, and link issues that can reduce job efficiency.

cards.title: Design priority
cards.body: Capacity planning focuses on predictable growth, redundancy, and general-purpose network refresh cycles.

cards.title: AI priority
cards.body: Design focuses on low latency, high radix, high fiber density, rapid turn-up, and fast service recovery.

slides.title: GPU Generations Drive Fiber Density

slides.title: Scale-Up, Scale-Out, and Scale-Across
slides.subtitle: The word scale hides three different optical design problems.

bullets.text: Tightly couples accelerators inside a rack or rack group, where connector access, packaging, and serviceability dominate.
Connects many systems into a larger training fabric, where switch radix, fiber count, and link reliability dominate.
Extends the fabric between rooms, halls, or nearby data-center buildings, where latency and reach become central.

figure.alt: Silent animation zooming from tightly coupled accelerator links inside a rack to a multi-rack fabric and then to optical links between nearby data-center buildings.
figure.caption: Scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across are nested domains, but each shifts the dominant optical constraint.

slides.title: Rack Architecture Becomes Part of the Network
slides.subtitle: Open Rack and wide rack designs make power, cooling, mechanical access, and fiber routing part of the same decision.

bullets.bold_lead: Rack width and power distribution matter
bullets.text: Wider equipment zones, 48V distribution, and high-density power shelves influence where optics and fibers can live.
Cold plates, manifolds, and blind-mate fluid connections compete for the same physical space as optical connectivity.

bullets.bold_lead: Optics must fit the maintenance workflow
bullets.text: A dense rack is only useful if technicians can replace trays and restore links without excessive fiber handling.

figure.alt: Flow showing power, cooling, optics, and operations as coupled rack-scale constraints.
figure.caption: Rack design increasingly couples power, cooling, optical routing, and service workflows.

slides.title: The Infrastructure Problem Is Bigger Than Bandwidth
items: AI fabrics push optics through several limits at once: speed, density, power, latency, and serviceability.
Scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across create different optical design pressures.
Rack architecture, cooling, and maintenance access now shape optical connectivity decisions.
Emerging optical technologies should be evaluated by the specific constraint they relieve.

slides.title: Check Your Model: AI Optical Drivers
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: What is the best reason AI clusters are driving denser optical infrastructure?

choices.text: AI fabrics need more high-speed, tightly coupled links across racks and systems.
AI clusters remove the need for switch-to-switch connectivity.
choices.feedback_incorrect: AI clusters usually increase high-speed connectivity. The pressure comes from more links, not fewer.

choices.text: Optical fibers are only needed for long-haul carrier networks.
choices.feedback_incorrect: In AI infrastructure, optical links are also used inside data centers and between nearby data-center buildings.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The scale and coupling of AI fabrics increase the number and importance of high-speed optical links.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the scale pattern. AI fabrics increase both link count and the operational importance of each link.

questions.prompt: Which statements correctly map design pressures to scaling patterns?

choices.text: Scale-up often stresses packaging, faceplate access, and serviceability.
Scale-out often stresses switch radix, fiber count, and link reliability.
Scale-across often makes latency and reach more important.
All three patterns are solved by the same connector change.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The scaling patterns overlap, but each one emphasizes a different part of the optical system.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Use the three-pattern model: scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across create related but distinct optical pressures.

slides.title: Design Pressures You Will Classify
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to:
objectives: Explain why electrical reach, faceplate space, and thermal load are pushing optics closer to the switch ASIC.
Describe the basic difference between pluggable, near-packaged, and co-packaged optics.
Explain how expanded beam connectivity changes dust sensitivity and maintenance workflows.
Identify the serviceability tradeoffs that come with denser optical integration.

slides.title: The Pluggable Model Hits System Limits
slides.subtitle: Front-panel optics remain useful, but high-radix AI switches expose their constraints.

bullets.text: Longer high-speed electrical paths need more signal conditioning, which increases power and heat.
Traditional cages consume the same front-panel space needed for denser fiber breakout.
Optics, cable bend radius, cooling, and technician access all compete at the front of the system.

figure.alt: Comparison of pluggable optics and near or co-packaged optics.
figure.caption: Moving optical conversion closer to the switching silicon can reduce electrical reach, but it changes the mechanical and service model.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 768 360" aria-label="Optical Conversion Moves Closer to the ASIC">

  <text x="205" y="170" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">Front panel</text><text x="205" y="194" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">longer electrical path</text><text x="205" y="218" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">easy replacement</text>

slides.title: Beyond Perimeter Escape: Areal Packaged Optics

figure.alt: Simplified diagram comparing perimeter escape, where optical I/O leaves around the die edge, with APO area escape, where optical I/O leaves through the die face to an optical engine under the die.
figure.caption: APO changes the scaling model: perimeter-based escape grows with edge length; area escape can grow with die face area.

slides.title: What CPO Is Trying to Save
slides.subtitle: The strongest CPO arguments are system arguments, not just component arguments.

bullets.text: Shorter electrical paths can reduce the signal-conditioning power associated with high-speed interfaces.
Less electrical power generally means less heat to remove near dense switching silicon.
Removing traditional front-panel transceiver cages opens new options for dense fiber connectivity.
Higher-density optical I/O can support larger fabrics with fewer tiers or less front-panel congestion.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: What CPO Makes Harder
slides.subtitle: Moving optics closer to the ASIC improves some constraints and sharpens others.

cards.title: Serviceability
cards.body: Replacing a failed optical function can become less straightforward than swapping a pluggable module.

cards.title: Laser strategy
cards.body: Systems may need clear choices for integrated, remote, or external laser sources.

cards.title: Thermal coupling
cards.body: Optical engines now live close to high-power silicon and tight cooling paths.

cards.title: Interoperability
cards.body: Form factors, electrical interfaces, fiber attach, and management models must mature together.

cards.title: Manufacturing scale
cards.body: Packaging precision, yield, and supply-chain readiness become central adoption factors.

cards.title: Operations model
cards.body: Teams need new playbooks for monitoring, repair, and sparing.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: From Package Integration to Field Interfaces
slides.subtitle: CPO changes where optics live; EBO changes how dense interfaces behave during service.

bullets.text: The package-level story is about shorter electrical reach, power, thermals, and denser optical I/O.

bullets.bold_lead: The fiber interface still has to survive operations
bullets.text: Dense racks still need connectors that can be mated, inspected, cleaned, replaced, and trusted by technicians.
Expanded beam optics, or EBO, uses lensing to make the mated optical path less sensitive to small contaminants.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Expanded Beam Optics (EBO) Reduces Cleanliness Sensitivity
slides.subtitle: EBO expands and collimates the light so a small contaminant blocks a smaller fraction of the signal path.

bullets.text: The fiber faces do not need to touch with the same precision as a physical-contact connector.
EBO makes the connection less sensitive to small particles at the mating interface.
The value is faster turn-up and recovery because technicians spend less time inspecting and recleaning connectors.

figure.alt: Silent animation comparing dust impact on physical-contact and expanded-beam optical connector paths.
figure.caption: Physical-contact connectors concentrate the optical spot at the mate plane; EBO spreads and collimates the light so the same contaminant blocks less of the path.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Why EBO Matters Beyond Dust
slides.subtitle: The driver is not only cleaner connectors; it is faster turn-up, denser service models, and fewer disturbed fibers.

bullets.text: At hyperscale, inspection and re-cleaning across millions of fibers can become schedule risk.
Non-contact lensed ferrules reduce contamination sensitivity, support dense arrays, and can lower mating force.
More repeatable optical mating makes tray, backplane, and rear-of-rack service models more practical.

slides.title: Rear Blind-Mate Builds on EBO and PTMT

figure.alt: Rear blind-mate deployment example with liquid, fiber, optical flex-plane, tray, blind-mate connector, and busbar elements.
figure.caption: This example shows rear blind mating as a system workflow: EBO and PTMT-style optical interfaces join liquid, power, tray, and busbar connections in a repeatable service motion.

slides.title: Efficiency Is a System Property
items: CPO targets electrical reach, power, thermals, and faceplate density.
Moving optics closer to silicon creates new serviceability and ecosystem maturity questions.
EBO and PTMT-style lensed ferrules target turn-up and recovery by reducing sensitivity to connector contamination.
Rear blind-mate designs convert that connector behavior into a tray-level maintenance workflow.

slides.title: Check Your Model: Efficiency vs Serviceability
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: What is the core reason CPO can reduce switch-system power?

choices.text: It shortens high-speed electrical paths between the ASIC and optical conversion.
It removes every optical connector from the system.
choices.feedback_incorrect: CPO changes where optical conversion happens, but systems still need fiber attachment and service interfaces.

choices.text: It makes the optical fiber itself consume less power.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The savings come mainly from reducing high-speed electrical reach and associated electronics, not from changing fiber power consumption.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. CPO reduces the electrical distance that high-speed signals must travel before becoming optical.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Focus on the electrical-to-optical boundary. CPO moves that boundary closer to the switching silicon.

questions.prompt: Which statements correctly describe EBO, PTMT-style interfaces, and rear blind-mate connectivity?

choices.text: EBO can reduce sensitivity to dust at the optical interface.
EBO and PTMT-style interfaces can make rear blind-mate optical connections more practical.
EBO means testing and insertion loss no longer matter.
Blind-mate serviceability must be designed with power and cooling interfaces too.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. These technologies target operational friction, but they still need disciplined link budgets and system design.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Separate the benefit from the exaggeration. EBO, PTMT-style interfaces, and blind mating can reduce service friction, but they do not eliminate optical engineering.

slides.title: Density Choices You Will Evaluate
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to:
objectives: Explain why fiber count becomes a physical constraint in trays, conduits, racks, and pathways.
Describe the basic idea of multicore fiber and space-division multiplexing.
Explain why fan-in and fan-out devices matter during ecosystem transition.
Compare testing implications for multicore and hollow-core fiber links.

NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Fiber Count Becomes a Physical Bottleneck
slides.subtitle: More bandwidth is useful only if the optical plant can be installed, routed, labeled, tested, and serviced.

bullets.text: Overhead trays, conduits, and rack managers have finite volume and bend-radius limits.
Very high strand counts increase cable weight and installation complexity.
Every additional connector, route, and test point becomes part of turn-up and maintenance.
A smaller cable is not enough if failures become harder to isolate.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Multicore Fiber Uses Space-Division Multiplexing

figure.alt: Diagram comparing single-core fiber and multicore fiber with cross-section and side-view light paths.
figure.caption: Single-core fiber carries one optical path; multicore fiber places multiple independent cores inside a common cladding for higher path density.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: 4-Core MCF Targets Density Without Rebuilding Everything
slides.subtitle: The emerging direction is not only a fiber design; it is a connectivity ecosystem problem.

bullets.text: Four cores can carry multiple independent optical paths in the same basic fiber outline.
Transceivers, connectors, fiber-array units, fan-outs, and test equipment must align.
Existing single-core ecosystems may still need fan-in and fan-out at the edge of the MCF link.

figure.alt: Chart comparing cable diameter and space savings for single-core and multicore fiber examples.
figure.caption: This chart illustrates the density argument for multicore fiber: more cores can be carried in less duct and tray space.

slides.title: Fan-In/Fan-Out Bridges Old and New Ecosystems

figure.alt: Silent animation showing four color-coded single-core optical paths mapped through fan-in into separate cores within one multicore fiber trunk, then restored through fan-out.
figure.caption: Fan-in and fan-out preserve core identity while a compact MCF trunk carries four optical paths through one shared fiber structure.

slides.title: CPO Harnesses Increase the Value of Compact Fiber Routing
slides.subtitle: Dense optical I/O close to silicon makes compact, well-managed fiber routing more valuable.

bullets.text: CPO and dense optical engines can place hundreds or thousands of optical paths near a switch package.
Fiber-array units, pitch conversion, and routing modules become part of the system design.

bullets.bold_lead: The plant and the package meet
bullets.text: A dense trunk strategy must connect cleanly to board-level and package-level optical interfaces.

figure.alt: Flow from optical engine to fiber-array unit, harness, and trunk.
figure.caption: The closer optics move to silicon, the more important compact and repeatable harnessing becomes.

slides.title: Testing Must Follow the New Failure Modes
slides.subtitle: Emerging fiber types can reuse some existing methods, but the inspection and characterization assumptions change.
rows: Inspect multiple cores and core positions; algorithms may need to see all cores together.
Direct HCF end-face inspection may need new algorithms when pigtails are not used.
Fan-out or hybrid cables can bridge to current loss testers, but one-cord referencing may need new modules.
Pigtails can use current tools; direct HCF connector testing needs method updates.
Existing OTDRs may work through fan-outs or hybrids, with attention to mapping.
Bidirectional OTDR and special analysis can matter because transitions and bends behave differently.
CD, PMD, and attenuation profile remain relevant for high-speed links.
Attenuation profile matters because HCF designs can be wavelength-optimized.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Density Gains Need Ecosystem Readiness
items: Fiber density is a physical installation and operations problem, not only a bandwidth problem.
Multicore fiber can reduce bulk by carrying multiple cores inside one fiber structure.
Fan-in and fan-out devices help bridge existing single-core endpoints to MCF trunks.
Testing methods must evolve so denser links remain diagnosable and certifiable.

slides.title: Check Your Model: Density and Testing
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: Why can multicore fiber reduce cable-plant pressure?

choices.text: It places multiple independent cores inside one shared fiber structure.
It removes the need to test optical links.
choices.feedback_incorrect: MCF changes density, but links still need inspection, certification, and troubleshooting.

choices.text: It makes every transceiver natively compatible without bridge devices.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Near-term deployments may still need fan-in and fan-out devices while the ecosystem matures.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. MCF addresses physical density by putting multiple cores inside a shared fiber outline.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Focus on spatial density. MCF reduces physical bulk, but it does not eliminate link engineering.

questions.prompt: Which testing considerations are reasonable for emerging MCF and HCF deployments?

choices.text: MCF inspection may need to evaluate multiple cores on one end face.
HCF links may need special OTDR analysis and attention to wavelength behavior.
Fan-out cables can help bridge MCF links to existing test equipment.
Once fiber density improves, link characterization is no longer useful.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Density improvements must be paired with inspection and characterization methods that fit the fiber type.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The right model is density plus diagnosability. New fiber structures still need trustworthy testing.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Emerging Maturity You Will Compare
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to:
objectives: Explain why hollow-core fiber can reduce propagation latency compared with conventional solid-core fiber.
Describe the plain-language idea behind anti-resonant hollow-core guidance.
Identify where lower latency matters in scale-across AI infrastructure.
Explain why QPUs are best understood as specialized accelerators in a hybrid compute stack.
Compare emerging optical technologies by adoption horizon and ecosystem readiness.

slides.title: Hollow-Core Fiber Changes the Medium
slides.subtitle: The core idea is simple: guide more of the light through air instead of solid glass.

bullets.text: Light travels closer to its vacuum speed in air than it does in silica glass.
Lower propagation delay matters most when distance is large enough to affect job timing or service placement.
Low latency is valuable only if the fiber can be produced, connected, tested, and operated at scale.

figure.alt: Hollow-core fiber visuals showing DNANF structure, leakage-loss diagram, and historical HCF design examples.
figure.caption: These images show the hollow-core structures behind the latency discussion and why modern DNANF designs are different from earlier HCF approaches.

extract_from.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Anti-Resonant Guidance in Plain Language

figure.alt: Silent conceptual animation showing a light pulse concentrated in the air-filled core of a hollow-core fiber while nested thin-walled glass membranes create the anti-resonant boundary.
figure.caption: The nested glass microstructure limits coupling into the cladding so the guided optical mode remains concentrated in the hollow air core.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Where Lower Latency Matters
slides.subtitle: HCF is most compelling when distance and synchronization are part of the performance problem.

bullets.text: Nearby buildings or campuses can become more practical when propagation delay is lower.
Collective operations are sensitive to slow links because many accelerators wait for shared results.
Lower latency can expand the practical radius for siting capacity near available power.

bullets.bold_lead: Latency is not the only metric
bullets.text: Loss, bend sensitivity, connectorization, splicing, test methods, and cost still determine deployability.

slides.title: HCF Promise vs Deployment Friction
slides.subtitle: The chart helps separate promising physics from deployment readiness.

figure.caption: This chart anchors the promise-versus-friction discussion in measured loss behavior across wavelengths.

bullets.text: Air-guided propagation can reduce delay compared with conventional solid-core fiber.
Recent hollow-core designs show much better attenuation than earlier structures.
Complex microstructured fiber must be produced repeatably and economically.
Splicing, connectorization, bend behavior, inspection, and testing methods still need maturity.

slides.title: From Propagation Speed to Specialized Compute
slides.subtitle: The module now shifts from moving data faster to applying specialized computation, using the same systems-readiness lens.

bullets.text: HCF targets propagation delay in the physical link; quantum systems target specialized computation.
The component alone is not the system. Connectivity, control, software, operations, and reliability determine practical value.
Treat the QPU as a specialized accelerator, then compare its adoption readiness with nearer-term optical technologies.

slides.title: CPU, GPU, and QPU: Different Engines for Different Work
slides.subtitle: The useful mental model is specialization, not replacement.

cards.title: CPU
cards.body: General orchestration, control flow, operating systems, and broad application logic.

cards.title: GPU
cards.body: Highly parallel deterministic math, tensor operations, simulation workloads, and acceleration.

cards.title: QPU
cards.body: Specialized quantum circuits for narrow problem classes where quantum behavior is the useful resource.

source_refs.source: NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx
NEW - OCP Academy - Emerging Technologies Slides_DRAFT_R 4.pptx

slides.title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Systems Need Low-Latency Integration
slides.subtitle: A QPU becomes useful in a data-center context when it can work as part of a tightly controlled classical system.

bullets.text: CPUs and GPUs prepare work, run control loops, and process results.
QPUs execute quantum circuits that are difficult to simulate classically.
Calibration, control, and error-correction workflows depend on fast feedback between classical and quantum resources.

figure.alt: Flow showing CPU, GPU, QPU, and feedback in a hybrid control loop.
figure.caption: Hybrid systems rely on fast feedback between classical accelerators and quantum processors.

figure.caption: Use adoption horizons as planning categories, not fixed promises. Ecosystem readiness can move technologies earlier or later.

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 768 360" aria-label="Emerging Optics Adoption Horizon">
  <rect width="768" height="360" fill="#fff"/>
  <text x="384" y="48" text-anchor="middle" font-size="26" font-weight="800" fill="#343895">Emerging Optics Adoption Horizon</text>

  <circle cx="90" cy="160" r="16" fill="#343895"/><text x="90" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Now</text><text x="90" y="242" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">800G</text><text x="90" y="266" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">density pressure</text><circle cx="285" cy="160" r="16" fill="#5F6062"/><text x="285" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Near</text><text x="285" y="242" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">CPO</text><text x="285" y="266" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">expanded beam</text><circle cx="480" cy="160" r="16" fill="#8DC63F"/><text x="480" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Next</text><text x="480" y="242" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">MCF</text><text x="480" y="266" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">HCF pilots</text><circle cx="675" cy="160" r="16" fill="#343895"/><text x="675" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Later</text><text x="675" y="242" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">hybrid</text><text x="675" y="266" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">QPU systems</text>
  <text x="384" y="325" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" fill="#5F6062">Each generation increases the amount of optical connectivity the physical plant must support.</text>
</svg>

slides.title: The Future Is a Portfolio, Not a Single Technology
items: HCF targets propagation latency and can expand design options for distance-sensitive fabrics.
QPU integration is a specialized compute topic that depends on low-latency classical control loops.
CPO, expanded beam, MCF, HCF, and QPU integration solve different constraints.
Adoption depends on physics, packaging, standards, supply chain, testability, and operations readiness.

slides.title: Check Your Model: Latency and Maturity
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry after feedback.

questions.prompt: Why can hollow-core fiber reduce propagation latency?

choices.text: It guides light mostly through air, where it travels faster than in solid glass.
It removes every source of optical loss and reflection.
choices.feedback_incorrect: HCF can reduce latency, but loss, reflection, bending, and connectorization still matter.

choices.text: It converts optical signals into electrical signals sooner.
choices.feedback_incorrect: HCF is about the optical propagation medium, not earlier electrical conversion.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. HCF lowers propagation delay by changing the medium that carries most of the light.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Focus on the medium. HCF changes where light travels inside the fiber.

questions.prompt: Which mappings correctly connect technology to primary pressure?

choices.text: CPO primarily addresses electrical reach, power, thermal, and faceplate constraints.
Expanded beam primarily addresses connector contamination sensitivity and service workflows.
MCF primarily addresses physical fiber density and cable bulk.
QPU integration primarily replaces all CPUs and GPUs in AI systems.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Each technology has a different center of gravity, even though they interact in real systems.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Classify by the main pressure each technology relieves. QPUs are specialized accelerators, not blanket replacements.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing the final module: Latency, Quantum, and the Adoption Horizon.
slides.cert_message: You have completed the course. You can now connect emerging optical technologies to the infrastructure pressures they are meant to relieve: scale, efficiency, density, latency, serviceability, and specialized compute integration.
